trajectory_summary: This call booked a qualified appointment on a warm, self-motivated lead with a large home, high bill, and EV charging load — the structural outcome is good. But the energy shape is flat: the rep ran a clean checklist on a customer who was ready to be excited, and left the call at roughly the same temperature it started. The Tesla/Powerwall cross-sell was noted but not developed, the bill was captured three times and flipped zero times, and the wife-on-title was acknowledged without a spouse-inclusion protocol.
outcome_dispute: The call booked an appointment — that outcome is real. However, the appointment is two weeks out, the wife (who is the title holder) was not confirmed as a participant, and the button-up omitted the phone-appointment specification, the no-driving anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. The sit is at structural risk on two fronts: decision-maker exclusion and weak confirmation protocol. The v1 tag of 'unknown' is defensible; if it was tagged as a clean book, that would warrant dispute.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Interest-in-solar depth: the emotional anchor the rep skipped
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I just got a Tesla. And as I was thinking about it, I was like, hey. What if I just charge this off of the sun? And then, you know, we got a huge roof line.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Kyle handed the rep the codex's designated emotional anchor — a specific, personal, high-value motivation — and the rep spent roughly 10 seconds on it before moving to Google Maps. Everything that followed (flat bill-flip, thin curiosity creation, no Powerwall bridge) traces back to this moment. The rep never built the emotional foundation that would have made the bill numbers land as pain rather than data. This is the root cause of the call's stagnant energy shape, and it's the highest-leverage coaching beat for this rep.

key_moments.quote: I just got a Tesla. And as I was thinking about it, I was like, hey. What if I just charge this off of the sun? And then, you know, we got a huge roof line. You'll see it. We've got, you know, a van. I mean, it's, like, six or 7,000 square feet.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kyle delivers the codex's designated emotional anchor — interest_in_solar — with a specific, personal, high-value motivation: a new Tesla, home charging intent, and a 6,000-7,000 sq ft home. The rep's response ('That's exactly what we'll be able to show you') acknowledges the answer but does not expand it. Per verify.interest_in_solar, 'depth matters more than the answer' and 'top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' The rep spent approximately 10 seconds before pivoting to Google Maps. This is the primary verify failure on the call — not an understandable miss given the customer's openness, but the most costly one.

key_moments.quote: 4,000 kilowatt hours is there after the year. I think we're paying, like, 15¢ kilowatt hours, so 500 something bucks, 600.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kyle self-calculates a $500-600/month bill and hands it to the rep. The rep responds 'Okay. Gotcha. Sounds good.' and moves to confirm the kWh average. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the required pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — the rep executed 'ask + acknowledge + move on,' which the codex identifies as the universal floor failure. The flip opportunity here was unusually high-leverage: Kyle is already paying $500-600/month to Duke AND is about to add EV charging load, meaning his bill is structurally going up. The rep had the numbers to make that point and didn't.

key_moments.quote: Colder and hotter months are a little bit higher. Yeah. Milder months are a little bit lower. Gotcha. Okay. Sounds good. But 4 k is a good middle ground.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kyle elaborates on seasonal variation — a second, unprompted invitation to anchor bill pain. The rep's 'K. Perfect.' and immediate pivot to the credit qualifier treats this as a data-confirmation moment rather than an emotional one. This is the third bill-data touch (after 00:52 and 02:16) and the third time the rep collected without converting. The seasonal detail is particularly useful because it signals that Kyle's bill peaks in summer and winter — the exact months when solar's value proposition is highest.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. My wife. It's actually in her name, I think, officially.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kyle discloses that the home is in his wife's name — she is the title holder, not a co-owner. This is a harder version of the spouse-on-title scenario: the primary decision-maker is not on the call. The rep's response is 'Oh, okay. To look here. Yeah. Yeah. No problem at all.' and moves to utilities. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct response is to include the spouse in the appointment — 'What time works for both of you to be on the call together?' The rep did not execute this protocol. The appointment is booked with the non-title-holder, which is a structural vulnerability for the sit.
